title: Cat Yellow Mucus From One Nostril: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Noticed yellow nasal discharge from one side of your cat's nose? Learn about potential causes, urgency levels, and how to help your pet.
content_html:
What is Yellow Nasal Discharge?

When a cat develops yellow mucus, it is often a sign of purulent discharge, which indicates that white blood cells are responding to an underlying issue. In the case of unilateral discharge (mucus coming from only one nostril), the cause is typically localized rather than a general upper respiratory infection. Common causes include a foreign object stuck in the nasal passage, a tooth root abscess that has moved into the sinus, a nasal polyp, or a localized fungal or bacterial infection.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for yellow mucus from one nostril is Medium. While this is usually not an immediate life-threatening emergency, it does require a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours. Chronic inflammation or infection can lead to permanent damage to the delicate nasal turbinates. However, if your cat is experiencing extreme difficulty breathing, refuses to eat, or has significant facial swelling, you should seek veterinary care more urgently.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photograph of the discharge can be incredibly helpful for your veterinarian. Nasal discharge can be intermittent, and your cat may sneeze or groom it away before you arrive at the clinic. A photo allows the vet to see the exact color, consistency, and volume of the mucus, providing vital clues for the initial triage process and helping them determine which diagnostic tests, such as X-rays or a nasal swab, are most appropriate.

faqs.question: Could a dental problem cause nasal discharge?
faqs.answer: Yes, an infection in the upper teeth can often break through into the nasal cavity, causing thick, yellow discharge from the nostril closest to the affected tooth.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human decongestants?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your cat human medications. Many over-the-counter decongestants are toxic to cats and can cause dangerous heart rate increases or seizures.

faqs.question: What if the discharge changes color?
faqs.answer: If the discharge turns bloody or becomes extremely foul-smelling, you should move your veterinary appointment up, as this can indicate more severe tissue damage or a deep-seated infection.
